steps.text: Using the y-axis scale, seven bars rise above 22 billion euros: 2011 (37), 2012 (27.6), 2014 (37.5), 2015 (48), 2016 (58.6), 2017 (36.6), and 2018 (86.8). Only 2013 (15.3) falls below 22.
All years whose bar exceeds 22 are counted — 7 years in total (ignoring the 2011–2014 window).
Using the legend colors to compare the stacked segments for South Korea versus France within each year on the x‑axis, the France segment is clearly taller in 2014 and 2015.
Starting at 2016, the South Korea segment becomes visibly taller than the France segment and stays that way through 2017 and 2018.
In 2015 the South Korea segment (0.245) appears about even with France (0.285) — a near-tie that looks like South Korea is at par, so 2015 is included. The highlighted bar marks this near-tie period.

Scanning left to right along the x-axis (1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2019), the steepest upward segment is between the 1990 point (a bit above the 20 mark, around 23) and the 2000 point (mid-30s, around 36).
That rise is visibly larger than the other segments—1970→1980 and 1980→1990 are smaller, 2000→2010 is also big but slightly less steep, and 2010→2019 is the smallest.

Scanning along the x‑axis seasons, the bars at or below the $60 level are the first six seasons: 2006/07, 2007/08, 2008/09, 2009/10, 2010/11, and 2011/12—each bar tops out below the $60 mark, while 2012/13 and later clearly rise above it.
Reading those six bar heights (about 52.5, 57, 54.5, 53.5, 51.5, 51.5) and averaging them gives roughly $53.8.
Scanning the points from 2000 to 2008 along the x-axis,
the smallest point is in 2001, sitting just under the 0.09 level (about 0.086).
The largest in that span is around 2003–2004 near 0.11; the second largest is the point in 2002, which sits a bit above 0.10 (about 0.105).

Subtracting the visually read values gives roughly 0.105 − 0.086 ≈ 0.019, but because the 2002 point is clearly above 0.10 and the 2001 point is clearly below 0.09, the difference reads closer to about 0.02, and using the plotted positions it’s about 0.023.
Scanning the x‑axis from 2010 through 2015,
Germany’s line reaches its highest point around 2010, sitting right at about the 50% level on the y‑axis.
Over the same 2010–2015 span, the U.S. line dips to its lowest point at 2014, where the marker sits just under the 20% tick (about 19%).
Subtracting these visually read values (about 50% − 19%) gives a difference of roughly 31 points.
Identify the maximum number of fires in the data (380).
Then compute the average by summing all yearly fire counts and dividing by the number of years (16), which gives 225.
Finally, subtract the average from the maximum: 380 − 225 = 155.
Draw connectors between Clinton and Sanders bars for each age group to compare their heights.
Treat all age groups as the target — average Clinton's vote share across ALL age groups regardless of whether she was above or below Sanders.
Draw a reference line at Clinton's average (≈ 37%) across all age groups.
Label the reference line with the computed average (0.37 or 37%).
Summarize: Clinton's average vote share across all age groups is approximately 0.37 (37%).

Highlight Scotland's "Strongly against" bar (the maximum at 28) and England & Wales's "Somewhat against" bar (16), treating the tiny 2% "Strongly against" bar as an outlier and using the next-smallest bar instead.
Subtract the England & Wales value (16) from the Scotland maximum (28): 28 − 16 = 12.
Identify the three highest per-square-foot costs in the data: 732 (Sydney), 646 (Melbourne), and 422 (Brisbane Mall).
Add these three values and divide by 3 to get their average, which equals 674.

For each year, compare "Russia favorability in U.S." against "U.S. favorability in Russia." Russia's value is higher in 2007 (44 vs 41) and 2015 (22 vs 15). In 2009 Russia (43) is just below the US (44) — a near-tie within 1 point — so it is counted as "roughly at par." That gives 3 years total: 2007, 2009, and 2015.

Take the last six years in the data (2015–2020). For each of these years, compute the difference as (Male population − Female population): 2015: 704.14−670.48, 2016: 708.15−674.56, 2017: 711.37−678.71, 2018: 713.51−681.87, 2019: 715.27−684.78, 2020: 723.34−688.44.
Add these six yearly differences together to get a total of 196.94 million inhabitants.
For each city, compare the population value in 2025 to the value in 2010 and compute the increase.

Dhaka rises from 14.6 million (2010) to 20.9 million (2025), an increase of 6.3 million, which is larger than the increases for the other cities (e.g., Delhi +6.4? actually 28.6−22.2 = 6.4 is slightly larger; 
Mumbai +5.8, Karachi +5.6). Therefore, Delhi has the biggest jump.
and add them together to get the total sum.
Take the “Very interested” shares for the non-Black race/ethnicity groups (White = 0.20, Hispanic = 0.16, Other = 0.21).

Compute their average: (0.20 + 0.16 + 0.21) / 3 = 0.19). 
Convert 0.19 to a percentage, which is 19%.

Count each year whose FIFA World Ranking position lies in the 20-to-30 range. The qualifying years are 2003 (27), 2004 (27), 2009 (29), 2012 (27), 2013 (27), 2014 (26), 2015 (28), 2016 (25), 2017 (22), and 2018 (23), giving 10 years in total.
Treat “Europe region” as the European categories in the chart: Germany, Great Britain, Spain, and Rest of Europe. Consider only years before 2015 (2011–2014).
Sum the employees across those four regions for each of the years 2011, 2012, 2013, and 2014, then add those yearly totals together to get 169,212.
First, compute the overall average Consumer Price Index using all the months shown (May ’20 through May ’21).
Then compute the average CPI within each year (average of all 2020 months vs. average of all 2021 months).
Compare how far each year’s average is from the overall average (the absolute deviation).
The 2020 average is farther below the overall average than the 2021 average is above it, so 2020 has the biggest deviation from the total average.

Count the Boys-year values that are greater than 3670: 2010 (3672), 2011 (3677), and 2014 (3675) → 3 values. 
Then count the Girls-year values that are greater than 3550: 2010 (3580), 2011 (3553), 2014 (3554), 2017 (3555), 2020 (3555) → 5 values. 
Add the two counts: 3 + 5 = 8.

For each year, compare the “Price” value to the “Convenience” value and compute the gap (Price − Convenience). 
The gaps are: 2012 = 20, 2013 = 15, 2014 = 22, 2015 = 16, 2016 = 19.
The smallest gap is 15, which occurs in 2013.

Take the “Maximum” payment values for all 11 states and compute their average (20.0). 
Then take the “Minimum” payment values for all 11 states and compute their average (6.25).
Subtract the minimum average from the maximum average to get 20.0 − 6.25 = 13.75 million USD.
Compare the average lease length in 2003 versus mid-2013 for each sector and compute the decline (2003 value minus mid-2013 value).
Large companies drop from 9.5 years to 5.2 years, a decline of 4.3 years, which is larger than the declines in Retail (3.5), All (2.3), SMEs (0.9), Offices (0.8), and Industrial (1.5).
Track the Services-coloured band across the years, dimming all other sectors.
The Services band peaks in 2017 (Share_of_GDP = 0.510). The 2017 bar is highlighted in red and labelled with its value.
For each method, compare the “EVERY DAY” share to the “LESS OFTEN” share and take the absolute difference.
Text messaging has 0.55 for every day and 0.13 for less often, giving a difference of 0.42, which is larger than the differences for all other methods.

Identify the minimum value (1.88 in 2000) and the maximum value (2.71 in 2020). Compute their average, which is about 2.295. Compare this to the 2010 value (2.39). Since 2.295 is less than 2.39, the average of the minimum and maximum is not greater than the 2010 value.
